summary: Nadia Asparouhova responds to an Atlantic article arguing that programmers shouldn't call themselves engineers. While she agrees that many developers today simply connect prefabricated code, she argues there exists a 'quiet class' of true software engineers who build and maintain digital infrastructure. Using OpenSSL and the Heartbleed bug as her central example, she shows how a tiny team of unpaid volunteers maintained code used by two-thirds of the world's web servers. These infrastructure maintainers exhibit the same pride, responsibility, and pressure as civil engineers, yet remain invisible and unappreciated until something goes wrong.
key_points: Digital infrastructure exists alongside physical infrastructure - the layers of tools, languages, libraries and frameworks that make modern programming easy
OpenSSL, used by two-thirds of web servers, was maintained by 11 volunteers with less than $2,000 in annual donations before Heartbleed
Infrastructure maintainers work under immense pressure knowing their code is used by banks, weapons systems, and governments worldwide
Tech media perpetuates stereotypes of overpaid developers while ignoring those 'toiling thanklessly under the hood'
The ease of modern programming is itself evidence of the invisible engineers who made it possible
money_quotes: Digital infrastructure may not be as permanent as physical infrastructure. It may change frequently. But millions of people still depend on digital infrastructure to function.
These tools are actively maintained by volunteers who work relentlessly, without pay, to ensure a programmer's favorite Python http library doesn't break.

It takes nerves of steel to work for many years on hundreds of thousands of lines of very complex code, with every line of code you touch visible to the world, knowing that code is used by banks, firewalls, weapons systems, web sites, smart phones, industry, government, everywhere.
Knowing that you'll be ignored and unappreciated until something goes wrong.
It's become acceptable in tech media to portray software developers as self-entitled, overpaid neckbeards.

If Bogost is correct, and today's computer programmers don't really do that much complicated work, then we must acknowledge there is still a quiet class of true 'software engineers' who are working hard to make it easy on the rest of us.
They're making it so easy for us, in fact, that we forget they're even there.
key_insight: The very ease of modern software development proves the existence of true software engineers - the invisible infrastructure maintainers whose thankless work makes everyone else's job look simple.
